title: Best Half Marathon Running Shoes
subtitle: The best half marathon running shoe depends on your weekly mileage, pace goals and the kind of session you're heading out for. A reliable daily trainer should be your first step. Add specialist shoes as your training builds.
metaTitle: Best Half Marathon Running Shoes 2026 | The Run Down
metaDescription: Expert-tested half marathon shoe picks for every runner profile. Race-day carbon, tempo shoes, and daily trainers for 21.1km. Updated May 2026.
whoThisIsFor: This page is for runners training for or racing a half marathon — from first-timers stepping up from a 10K to experienced runners chasing a sub-90-minute finish. 21.1km is the distance where speed matters as much as durability. We cover everything from daily trainers to full carbon race shoes.

editorialBoxes.heading: Cushion vs Speed: What actually matters for a half?
editorialBoxes.body: 21.1km is the distance where speed wins. You're on your feet long enough for foam quality to matter, but not so long that maximum stack pays off the way it does in a full. The sweet spot for most half-marathoners is a mid-to-high stack shoe with a carbon or nylon plate — light enough to hold goal pace, cushioned enough to protect through the final 5km.

editorialBoxes.heading: How to read a shoe spec sheet
editorialBoxes.body: Weight, drop, stack height, and foam type are the four numbers that matter most. Weight affects effort over distance — every 100g adds ~1% energy cost. Drop (heel-to-toe offset) affects foot strike and calf load. Foam type determines energy return — PEBA-based foams significantly outperform older EVA foams.

editorialBoxes.heading: How your runner profile should shape your choice
editorialBoxes.body: A new runner needs forgiveness and confidence — a plush neutral trainer with no plate. A PB chaser needs precision — a race-ready carbon shoe with a fast training companion. A long runner needs protection over hours — maximum stack, durable foam, and a shoe that doesn't break down at mile 20.

faqs.question: Do I need different shoes for training and race day?
faqs.answer: Short answer: yes, if you're targeting a time. Race shoes use carbon plates and exotic foams that excel for a handful of halves before degrading; training shoes are built to absorb hundreds of kilometres. Rotating both extends the life of your race pair and builds stronger legs.

faqs.question: Are carbon plated shoes worth it for beginners?
faqs.answer: For a first half marathon, probably not. The benefit is real but small, and the stiff ride can amplify form issues over 21.1km. Spend the money on a forgiving, well-cushioned trainer and a structured plan instead.

faqs.question: How often should I replace my half marathon shoes?
faqs.answer: Training shoes: 500–800km depending on weight and surface. Race shoes: 200–400km — foam compression and plate fatigue come well before visible wear.

faqs.question: What's the difference between a stability and neutral half marathon shoe?
faqs.answer: Stability shoes guide the foot against excess inward roll (overpronation) using firmer foam on the medial side or a guide rail. Neutral shoes let the foot move naturally. Most runners are neutral; if you're not sure, you probably are.

faqs.question: Is a higher stack height always better for a half?
faqs.answer: Up to a point. Over 21.1km your legs hold up better than in a full, so a slightly lower-stack shoe (28–36mm) can give you a sharper, more connected ride without the cost. If you tend to fade in the back 5km, lean higher.

author: ashley-morgan
